talk_metadata.talk_title: A Targetable Metabolite Increases Neuronal Hyperexcitability in a Glioma Microenvironment and Directs Tumor Growth
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we haven't gotten there yet" when asked about immunocompetent models
Speaker said: "I can't quite pinpoint it yet in terms of gabapentin" regarding gabapentin interpretation

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: AGAT knockout reduces GAA secretion but does not affect tumor growth in extracranial sites, indicating limited impact on systemic disease

trial_dilution_risk.trial_name_or_nct: Surgical window of opportunity trial (12 patients)
trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: Modulation of GAA via ornithine supplementation and arginine-restricted diet
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: Small sample size (12 patients) limits efficacy conclusions

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Relies on patient adherence to restrictive diet and supplementation, which may be difficult to standardize
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Broad enrollment without molecular selection may dilute signal

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that GAA knockout improves mouse survival but then said "they grow normally in a dish" suggesting in vitro fitness is unchanged despite in vivo benefit

key_data_gaps: No comparison to standard of care

crispro_opportunity.description: Identify patients with high intratumoral GAA levels to enrich for responders to ornithine/arginine diet
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "we validated this with two additional independent cohorts" and "GAA is increased in high-grade glioma versus brain"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate metabolomic and transcriptomic data to develop a GAA‑high signature for patient selection
external_validation_needed: ClinicalTrials.gov: NCTxxxx for the surgical window trial
PubMed: studies linking GAA levels to glioma outcomes

crispro_opportunity.description: Combine dietary GAA modulation with existing glutamate‑targeting agents (e.g., glutaminase inhibitors) to enhance tumor control
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Audience question about combining with pegarginase and speaker noted linear pathway similarity
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model metabolic flux interactions to predict synergistic combinations
external_validation_needed: Preclinical data on glutaminase inhibition in glioma

cited_competitors.name: pegarginase
cited_competitors.context: Discussed as a potential combination with GAA modulation

unresolved_questions: What is the mechanism of GAA‑induced neuronal hyperexcitability at the molecular level?
Will long‑term dietary restriction of arginine affect systemic metabolism or cause adverse effects?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Targeting Mitochondrial Complex I Overcomes Adaptive Response to Chemotherapy in Advanced Pancreatic Cancer
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we never work on the resistance because we are still working on..."
Speaker said: "IM accumulates based on potential, so maybe it's less toxic"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: IX caused lactic acidosis due to potent inhibition of complex I without reversible accumulation
vulnerability_identified.hidden_tox_signal: Speaker minimized toxicity by stating IM is less potent but less toxic

trial_dilution_risk.trial_name_or_nct: Phase 1/2 MD Anderson trial of lixumustat (IM-156) + gemcitabine/nab‑paclitaxel
trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: Inhibit mitochondrial complex I to enhance chemotherapy
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: No biomarker (e.g., OXPHOS high signature) used to select patients
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: Small cohort (18 patients) limits statistical power

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker disclosed that IM accumulates based on mitochondrial membrane potential, suggesting a reversible, less potent mechanism that may be circumvented by tumors with altered potential
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Broad dosing without patient stratification may dilute efficacy signal

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed preclinical data that IM eradicates relapse clones but concluded that the combination is safe despite limited long‑term toxicity data

crispro_opportunity.description: Incorporate OXPHOS‑high biomarker to enrich for patients likely to benefit from complex I inhibition
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker noted a signature that predicts sensitivity to chemotherapy and OXPHOS upregulation
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can generate and validate an OXPHOS activation score for trial stratification

crispro_opportunity.description: Leverage reversible, potential‑dependent accumulation of IM to claim a safety advantage over earlier complex I inhibitors
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "IM accumulates based on potential, so maybe it's less toxic"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model mitochondrial potential dynamics to predict differential toxicity across compounds
external_validation_needed: Preclinical studies comparing IM vs IX toxicity in high‑potential models

cited_competitors.name: IX (previous complex I inhibitor)
cited_competitors.context: Discussed as discontinued due to toxicity

unresolved_questions: What resistance mechanisms will emerge to IM‑based combination therapy?
Can IM be combined safely with KRAS inhibitors?

companies_to_monitor: Companies developing reversible complex I inhibitors

talk_metadata.institution_or_pharma: Mays Cancer Center at UT Health
talk_metadata.talk_title: Estrogen Therapy Alleviates Cancer‑Associated Cachexia in Mouse Models of Both Sex
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we believe this kind of new therapies maybe can help the cancer patient beyond kidney cancer"
Speaker said: "we don't know which estrogen receptor is responsible"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Rezotibib reduces EPO production, worsening anemia, which was not anticipated in the cachexia model
vulnerability_identified.hidden_tox_signal: Speaker noted anemia worsened, indicating off‑target endocrine effect

trial_dilution_risk.trial_name_or_nct: Preclinical mouse studies of estrogen pellets
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: No stratification by tumor genotype (e.g., LKB1 status) or sex hormone baseline
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: Small group sizes, no power calculation

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: E2 is a generic hormone; formulation can be easily replicated, limiting proprietary advantage
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Broad hormonal therapy may have systemic side effects and lacks patient selection

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that HIF2α inhibition rescues muscle/fat loss but then admitted it worsens anemia, contradicting the claim of overall benefit without adjunct therapy

key_data_gaps: No long‑term safety of estrogen in male patients
No mechanistic dissection of ERα vs ERβ

crispro_opportunity.description: Develop a transcriptomic signature of HIF‑driven cachexia to identify patients who may benefit from estrogen therapy
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker presented RNA‑seq showing up‑regulation of hypoxia genes in KPL tumors
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate tumor and peripheral tissue RNA‑seq to generate a cachexia risk score
external_validation_needed: Clinical datasets linking HIF signatures to cachexia outcomes

crispro_opportunity.description: Combine estrogen therapy with HIF2α inhibition plus EPO support to mitigate anemia
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker noted need for EPO transfusion when using rezotibib
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model multi‑agent metabolic interactions to predict synergistic windows
external_validation_needed: Trials of HIF2α inhibitors with erythropoiesis agents

cited_competitors.name: rezotibib
cited_competitors.context: HIF2α inhibitor used in preclinical cachexia model

unresolved_questions: Which estrogen receptor subtype mediates the anti‑cachexia effect?
Will estrogen therapy have adverse effects in male cancer patients?

talk_metadata.talk_title: A Ketogenic Diet Sensitizes Pancreatic Cancer to Metabolic Therapies
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we still have tumor in the mice after 227 days"
Speaker said: "we haven't looked at immune cell localization yet"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Tumors adapt by up‑regulating glutamine uptake and TCA cycle flux, bypassing glucose restriction
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: Speaker noted tumor persists after long‑term ketogenic diet

trial_dilution_risk.trial_name_or_nct: Preclinical mouse studies of ketogenic diet + glutamine inhibitors
trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: Exploit glutamine dependence induced by ketogenic diet
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: No patient biomarker (e.g., glutamine transporter expression) to select responders
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: Small animal cohort, no power analysis

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Dietary interventions are not patentable and can be replicated easily
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Patient adherence to strict macronutrient ratios is challenging

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that ketogenic diet raises glutamine levels yet concluded that diet alone is insufficient, highlighting a paradox between metabolic adaptation and therapeutic efficacy

crispro_opportunity.description: Identify tumors with high glutamine transporter (SLC1A5) expression to select for ketogenic‑diet plus glutamine inhibition
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker reported up‑regulation of glutamine uptake channels under ketogenic diet
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate RNA‑seq and metabolomics to generate a glutamine‑dependency score
external_validation_needed: Clinical studies of ketogenic diet adherence

crispro_opportunity.description: Combine ketogenic diet with CB‑839 (glutaminase inhibitor) to block adaptive glutamine utilization
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker used CBA39 and DON as glutamine inhibitors showing synergy
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model flux redistribution when glutaminase is inhibited under ketogenic conditions
external_validation_needed: Phase I trials of CB‑839 in PDAC

cited_competitors.name: CB‑839 (CBA39)
cited_competitors.context: Glutamine inhibitor combined with ketogenic diet

cited_competitors.name: DON
cited_competitors.context: Glutamine pathway inhibitor used in combination

unresolved_questions: How does the ketogenic diet affect immune cell function within the tumor microenvironment?
What is the relative contribution of glutamate versus glutamine to TCA cycle flux under diet

companies_to_monitor: Companies developing ketogenic diet clinical programs

talk_metadata.talk_title: Metabolic Changes Associated with Senescence in CAFs Regulate Fibrosis in Pancreatic Cancer
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we haven't looked at that yet" regarding AKG release
Speaker said: "we are currently targeting IDH2/IDH3"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Accumulation of α‑ketoglutarate in senescent CAFs may limit prolyl hydroxylase activity if not supplied, but the pathway's dependency is not yet proven

trial_dilution_risk.trial_name_or_nct: Preclinical mouse model using Ink‑Tag senescent cell depletion
trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: Eliminate senescent CAFs to reduce fibrosis
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: No patient biomarker (e.g., P16+ α‑SMA+ CAF) to select for senescent‑CAF‑targeted therapy
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: Mouse cohort size not disclosed, likely underpowered

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: IDH inhibitors are already in clinical use for other indications, reducing novelty
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Broad inhibition of IDH may affect normal cells and cause off‑target effects

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed increased α‑KG in senescent CAFs but concluded that targeting IDH2/3 will reduce collagen without demonstrating that α‑KG is the limiting substrate for hydroxylases

key_data_gaps: In vivo validation of IDH2/3 inhibition on tumor fibrosis
Human correlation of senescent CAF metabolic signature

crispro_opportunity.description: Develop a senescent‑CAF metabolic signature (high α‑KG, PKM2/1 ratio) to identify PDAC patients who may benefit from IDH inhibition
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker presented scRNA‑seq showing oxidative phosphorylation and glycolysis enrichment in senescent CAFs
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate single‑cell transcriptomics to derive a senescent‑CAF score for patient selection

crispro_opportunity.description: Repurpose existing IDH2/3 inhibitors for stromal targeting in PDAC
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker indicated ongoing work targeting IDH2/3 in senescent CAFs
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can predict off‑target effects and optimal dosing for stromal versus tumor compartments
external_validation_needed: Clinical trial data of IDH inhibitors in non‑hematologic cancers

unresolved_questions: Does inhibition of IDH2/3 in senescent CAFs affect tumor growth in vivo?
Is α‑KG accumulation extracellularly or retained within CAFs?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Targeting Metabolism in BRCA Deficient Tumors
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we observed an additive effect" when combining mitapivat with PARP inhibitors
Speaker said: "we haven't done the resistant models yet"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Lack of PKM1 upregulation in wildtype limits drug sensitivity
vulnerability_identified.ghost_responder_admission: Speaker noted selective cell death in BRCA2 knockout cells

trial_dilution_risk.trial_name_or_nct: Preclinical mouse study of mitapivat in BRCA‑deficient tumors
trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: Activate PKM2 to induce mitochondrial ROS in BRCA‑deficient cells
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: No biomarker beyond BRCA status; does not account for PKM1 expression heterogeneity

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Repurposed drug with known safety profile, limiting exclusivity for oncology
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Efficacy appears limited to BRCA‑deficient context; narrow patient pool

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that PKM2 activation causes mitochondrial dysfunction yet concluded that the drug is well‑tolerated with no weight loss in mice

key_data_gaps: No clinical data in BRCA‑mutated patients
Unclear mechanism linking PKM1 upregulation to ROS

crispro_opportunity.description: Develop a PKM1‑high signature to select BRCA‑mutant patients likely to respond to PKM activators
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker demonstrated upregulation of PKM1 in BRCA2 knockout tumors
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can combine RNA‑seq and metabolomics to predict PKM1‑driven ROS susceptibility
external_validation_needed: TCGA BRCA‑mutant tumor PKM1 expression data

crispro_opportunity.description: Combine mitapivat with PARP inhibitors to achieve additive anti‑tumor activity in BRCA‑deficient cancers
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker reported additive effect of mitapivat with PARP inhibition in vitro
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model synthetic lethality between PKM activation‑induced ROS and PARP blockade
external_validation_needed: Clinical trial of mitapivat + olaparib in BRCA‑mutant cohort

unresolved_questions: Will PKM1 overexpression alone be sufficient to sensitize BRCA‑wildtype tumors?
What is the role of mitophagy impairment in the observed cell death?
